title: All-purpose healing salve
subtitle: A beeswax and shea butter salve for dry skin, cracked heels, and minor irritation
excerpt: Beeswax gives this salve its firmness. Shea butter softens it and adds a long-lasting moisturising layer. Jojoba penetrates quickly. Lavender and tea tree essential oils are included at skin-safe levels for their soothing properties. The result is a multi-use balm that works on hands, cuticles, cracked heels, lips, and minor dry patches.
sourceNotes: Healing salve formulation from Nerys Purchon, Handbook of Natural Beauty (2001). EO dilution rates from Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014). Beeswax and shea butter ratios from Gracefruit cosmetic-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026).

recipe.yieldDescription: 2 small tins of salve (~60 g each, 120 g total)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Pour into small tins (30-60 ml) for gifting or travel. Larger tins stay soft in warm rooms.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Salves keep for 12 months and are an excellent ahead-of-time project. Make a batch and gift half.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt beeswax and shea butter over a water bath. Remove from heat before adding the liquid oils and essential oils. The mixture does not need to reach a specific temperature: melt until liquid, then cool to hand temperature before adding EOs.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool, dark place. No water content means no preservative is required. Beeswax and shea butter have long natural shelf lives. Vitamin E in the jojoba oil adds further stability. Discard if the salve smells rancid.

recipeTools.notes: Or a heat-proof jug; dedicated to cosmetic use

glossaryTerms.definition: A product that contains no water. Balms, salves, and body butters are anhydrous. Because water is absent, bacteria and fungi cannot grow and no preservative is required. An anhydrous formula cannot moisturise the skin by adding water: it works by forming a barrier that retains the skin's own moisture.

content.text: : it contains no water. This means bacteria and fungi cannot grow and no preservative is needed, which makes it one of the safest and longest-lasting things to make. The beeswax sets the salve firm enough to hold in a tin; the shea butter provides the skin-conditioning effect. Jojoba is the quickest-absorbing of the three oils and contributes to the overall penetration speed.

Lavender essential oil at 0.5% and tea tree at 0.3% are both well within safe skin-use limits. Tea tree should not be used around cats. For a lip-safe version, omit the tea tree essential oil entirely.

items.prepNote: 0.3% of batch weight; approximately 8 drops; omit for lip-safe version

content.text: Weigh the beeswax pellets, shea butter, and jojoba oil into a heat-proof jug. Melt over a simmering-water bath, stirring occasionally, until fully liquid. Remove from heat. Allow to cool until the base of the jug is comfortable to hold: around 40 to 45 degrees Celsius. Stir in the vitamin E oil, then the essential oils. Pour immediately into tins before the wax begins to set. Leave undisturbed at room temperature for 1 hour to set fully. Do not move the tins while the surface is still liquid or a rippled, uneven surface will form.

12 months at room temperature, away from direct sunlight. The salve hardens in cold conditions and softens in warm ones: this is normal. Scoop out with a clean spatula or clean fingers to avoid introducing moisture or bacteria into the tin.
